items.title: Threat Actors Target FIFA World Cup 2026
items.description: A sophisticated Chinese-origin fraud operation is targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees through pixel-perfect website clones and a multi-tenant phishing infrastructure. The actors deploy typosquatted domains and a commercially developed administrative system to mimic legitimate FIFA ticketing platforms. Technical analysis reveals high-fidelity brand cloning, real-time card skimming capabilities,
items.ai_summary: The threat actors behind the operation targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees utilize a combination of typosquatted domains and high-fidelity brand cloning to create pixel-perfect website clones, leveraging T1583.001 to register deceptive domains and T1566 for phishing attacks. The real-world impact of this operation includes potential financial loss for attendees who fall victim to the phishing campaign, with the threat actors capable of real-time card skimming. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on verifying website authenticity, and monitor for suspicious domain registrations. Specifically, defenders should configure their DNS servers to block known typosquatted domains and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent phishing attempts. The threat actors' use of commercially developed administrative systems suggests a high level of sophistication, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring and swift incident response.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.001| C[Resource Development]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion
items.description: Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels are utilizing sophisticated online infrastructure consisting of over 36 inauthentic websites to facilitate sanctions evasion. These websites impersonate ship registries, national maritime administrations, seafarer training organizations, protection and indemnity clubs, and classification societies from jurisdictions including Comoros, Benin, Bhutan, Cameroo
items.ai_summary: The Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion threat involves Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels utilizing over 36 inauthentic websites to impersonate legitimate maritime organizations, facilitating sanctions evasion. The attack vector involves social engineering tactics to deceive victims into using these fake websites, which can lead to compromised sensitive information and financial losses. The real-world impact of this threat is significant, as it enables sanctioned vessels to continue operating undetected, potentially disrupting global trade and security. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust domain validation and verification processes, ensuring that only legitimate websites are used for maritime-related activities. Additionally, regular monitoring of online infrastructure and swift takedown of inauthentic websites can help mitigate the threat.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1562| B[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1110| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Gamers beware: malicious wallpapers on Steam found stealing accounts
items.description: Since late 2025, cybercriminals have been exploiting Wallpaper Engine, a popular live wallpaper application on Steam, to distribute malware through Steam Workshop. Attackers target primarily Chinese and Russian gamers by embedding malicious code within application wallpapers shared on the platform. These compromised wallpapers deliver various malware types including infostealers, backdoors, crypto
items.ai_summary: The malicious wallpapers on Steam exploit the Wallpaper Engine application to distribute malware, primarily targeting Chinese and Russian gamers. The attackers embed malicious code within the wallpapers shared on the Steam Workshop platform, which delivers various malware types, including infostealers, backdoors, and cryptocurrency stealers. If exploited, the malware can lead to unauthorized access to the victim's Steam account, resulting in financial loss and sensitive information theft. To remediate this threat, Steam users should avoid downloading wallpapers from untrusted sources, and instead, opt for verified and trusted content creators. Additionally, users should regularly monitor their account activity and report any suspicious behavior to Steam support.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: WebAssembly Malware Found in Trojanized Open VSX Extensions
items.description: Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions ship ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that poll the Solana blockchain for command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos. This novel dead-drop technique allows attackers to rotate infrastructure without hardco
items.ai_summary: The threat involves Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace, which deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions contain ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that communicate with the Solana blockchain to receive command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos, utilizing the T1071 application layer protocol technique. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of developer systems, potentially affecting a wide range of industries that rely on Visual Studio Code. To remediate, developers should remove any suspicious extensions and update their Visual Studio Code to the latest version, ensuring that all extensions are verified and come from trusted sources. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for unusual Solana blockchain interactions can help in early detection of such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Public and Private Medical Community Targeted by Threat Actor Pursuing Artificial Intelligence, Cyber, Medical, and National Defense Research
items.description: A sophisticated espionage campaign attributed to UNC6508, a China-nexus threat actor, targeted North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year. The adversary exploited REDCap servers, deployed custom INFINITERED malware to harvest credentials, and maintained persistent access through trojanized legitimate files that survived software upgrades. After remaining u
items.ai_summary: The UNC6508 threat actor has been conducting a sophisticated espionage campaign targeting North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year, with a focus on artificial intelligence, cyber, medical, and national defense research. The adversary exploited REDCap servers, likely through social engineering or unpatched vulnerabilities, to gain initial access and deploy custom INFINITERED malware for credential harvesting. The malware was designed to maintain persistent access through trojanized legitimate files that survived software upgrades, allowing the threat actor to continue exfiltrating sensitive research data. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately inspect their REDCap servers for signs of compromise, update to the latest version, and implement robust access controls, including multi-factor authentication and regular password rotations. Additionally, defenders should conduct thorough network traffic analysis to detect and block any suspicious activity related to the INFINITERED malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How attackers are jailbreaking LLMs with CTF framing and how to catch them
items.description: Threat actors are bypassing AI model safety guardrails by framing exploit requests as legitimate security research, such as capture-the-flag challenges or CVE-hunting exercises. This technique manipulates upstream LLMs into generating working exploit code that attackers deploy against real targets. Multiple independent operators have been observed targeting five applications—PraisonAI, LiteLLM, Fa
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting large language models (LLMs) by framing malicious requests as capture-the-flag challenges or CVE-hunting exercises, effectively bypassing safety guardrails. This technique manipulates upstream LLMs into generating working exploit code, which is then deployed against real targets, including PraisonAI, LiteLLM, and Fa. The real-world impact of this exploit includes unauthorized access to sensitive data and potential disruption of services. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict input validation and monitoring of LLM-generated code, as well as provide security awareness training to researchers and developers to prevent accidental or intentional misuse. Additionally, organizations should review and update their AI model safety guardrails to prevent similar bypass attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Investigation of email-based attack delivering MediaFire ZIP file with execution chain analysis
items.description: An investigation revealed a malicious email campaign directing victims to download a ZIP file from MediaFire. The infection chain began with a Python setup executable (Setu.exe) that side-loaded a malicious 400 MB python37.dll containing repeated byte padding. The DLL performed process injection into dllhost.exe, establishing communication with a C2 server at 138.124.186.2:7000. The threat actor d
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a malicious email campaign that directs victims to download a ZIP file from MediaFire, which contains a Python setup executable (Setu.exe) that side-loads a malicious python37.dll. This DLL performs process injection into dllhost.exe, allowing the threat actor to establish communication with a C2 server at 138.124.186.2:7000. The attack vector involves phishing (T1566) and process injection (T1055), with the threat actor leveraging application layer protocol (T1071) for C2 communication. To remediate, defenders should block traffic to the C2 server IP and port, and implement endpoint detection and response tools to identify and mitigate process injection attempts. Additionally, users should be educated to avoid downloading executables from untrusted sources, and to verify the authenticity of emails before interacting with attachments or links.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1055| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Android Banker with Complete Device Takeover Capabilities
items.description: A newly identified Android banking trojan named Rokarolla has been discovered, distributed through malicious websites masquerading as popular applications like TikTok or Google Chrome. The malware targets 217 distinct cryptocurrency and banking applications using 137 sophisticated commands for device control. Capabilities include harvesting lock screen credentials, exfiltrating contact lists and S
items.ai_summary: The Rokarolla Android banking trojan is a sophisticated malware that targets 217 distinct cryptocurrency and banking applications, allowing for complete device takeover capabilities. The malware is distributed through malicious websites masquerading as popular applications like TikTok or Google Chrome, utilizing drive-by compromise (T1189) and masquerading (T1036) tactics. If exploited, the malware can harvest lock screen credentials, exfiltrate contact lists, and perform other malicious activities, affecting individuals and organizations that use the targeted applications. To remediate, users should avoid downloading applications from untrusted sources, keep their devices and applications up-to-date, and use anti-malware software to detect and remove the malware. Additionally, organizations can implement security measures such as mobile device management (MDM) and mobile application management (MAM) to protect against similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1189| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Potemkin Loader & RMMProject The Anatomy of a ClickFix Attack
items.description: A ClickFix social engineering attack on an unmonitored endpoint led to a multi-stage intrusion affecting over 11 hosts. The infection chain began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic DGA. Potemkin delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable RAT featuring browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encrypt
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix social engineering attack exploited an unmonitored endpoint, leading to a multi-stage intrusion that affected over 11 hosts. The attack began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA). This loader delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that featured browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encryption. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust endpoint monitoring, restrict user execution of unknown files, and enforce multi-factor authentication to prevent credential theft. Additionally, defenders should block all incoming traffic on unused ports and restrict outgoing traffic to only necessary services, and update their security software to detect and block Potemkin and RMMProject.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1555.003| E[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Attackers Weaponize Microsoft Teams Relays to Stay Hidden
items.description: Attackers deploying DragonForce ransomware against a major U.S. services firm concealed their command-and-control traffic within Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure using Backdoor.Turn, a custom Go-based remote access trojan. This novel technique leverages anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic. The intrusion lasted o
items.ai_summary: The attackers exploited the Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure to conceal their command-and-control traffic, utilizing a custom Go-based remote access trojan known as Backdoor.Turn. This technique leveraged anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic, allowing the attackers to remain hidden. The DragonForce ransomware was deployed against a major U.S. services firm, resulting in a significant intrusion that lasted for an undisclosed duration. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict traffic monitoring and filtering rules to detect and block suspicious Microsoft Teams relay traffic, and ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure communication protocols. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a security information and event management (SIEM) system to monitor for indicators of compromise associated with Backdoor.Turn and DragonForce ransomware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-11456: A vulnerability was identified in Chanjet CRM 1.0. This affects an unknown part ...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in Chanjet CRM 1.0. This affects an unknown part of the file /tools/jxf_dump_systable.php of the component HTTP GET Request Handler. Such manipulation of the argument gblOrgID leads to sql injection. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11456 is a SQL injection vulnerability in Chanjet CRM 1.0, specifically affecting the HTTP GET Request Handler component in the file /tools/jxf_dump_systable.php. The vulnerability can be exploited by manipulating the gblOrgID argument, allowing an attacker to inject malicious SQL code. This can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, potentially affecting all users of the CRM system. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to a patched version of Chanjet CRM, if available, and implement input validation and sanitization for the gblOrgID argument in the jxf_dump_systable.php file. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious database activity and consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent SQL injection attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-11455: A vulnerability was determined in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.2. Affected...
items.description: A vulnerability was determined in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.2. Affected by this issue is the function check_cmd_exists of the file metagpt/utils/common.py. This manipulation of the argument mermaid.path causes command injection. The attack may be initiated remotely. A high degree of complexity is needed for the attack. The exploitation is known to be difficult. The exploit has been public
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11455 is a command injection vulnerability in the FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to version 0.8.2, specifically affecting the check_cmd_exists function in the metagpt/utils/common.py file. The vulnerability can be exploited remotely with a high degree of complexity, making it difficult to exploit. If successfully exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to inject arbitrary commands, potentially leading to code execution and unauthorized access. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to MetaGPT version 0.8.3 or later, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, users can mitigate the risk by validating and sanitizing user input to prevent malicious command injection.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11453 is a SQL injection vulnerability in the Tiobon Employee Self-Service System up to version 7.2, specifically affecting the /Blog/BlogSearch.aspx file of the Login Endpoint. The vulnerability can be exploited remotely by manipulating the Keyword argument, allowing an attacker to inject malicious SQL code. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive employee data, potentially affecting all users of the system. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to Tiobon Employee Self-Service System version 7.3 or later, and consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent SQL injection attacks. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and consider conducting regular security audits to identify and address potential vulnerabilities.

items.description: A vulnerability has been found in GL.iNet GL-MT3000 up to 4.4.5. Affected is the function FUN_0042e200 of the file /cgi-bin/glc of the component SET_USER_PWD Handler. The manipulation of the argument Password leads to command injection. The attack can be initiated remotely. Upgrading to version 4.8.1 is able to address this issue. The affected component should be upgraded. The vendor explains: " T
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11452 is a command injection vulnerability in the GL.iNet GL-MT3000 router, specifically in the SET_USER_PWD Handler component, affecting versions up to 4.4.5. The vulnerability can be exploited remotely by manipulating the Password argument in the FUN_0042e200 function of the /cgi-bin/glc file, allowing an attacker to inject malicious commands. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to gain unauthorized access to the router, potentially leading to further malicious activities such as data theft or lateral movement within a network. To remediate this issue, defenders should upgrade their GL.iNet GL-MT3000 routers to version 4.8.1, which addresses the vulnerability. Additionally, defenders should ensure that their routers are configured to only allow remote access from trusted sources and that strong passwords are used for all user accounts.

items.title: Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting
items.description: A flaw in the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python let an attacker with no access to a victim's project hijack the victim's machine learning model upload and run code inside Google's serving infrastructure. Palo Alto Networks Unit 42, which found and reported the bug through Google's bug bounty program, calls the technique "Pickle in the Middle" and said it saw no exploitation in the wild.

Attacks involving BabaDeda Loader, observed in April 2026, have targeted education and financial organizations. "Earlier BabaDeda activity was known for

items.title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
items.description: The emerging malware, spread via fake TikTok and Chrome downloads, demonstrates an evolution by combining banking fraud with extensive device surveillance and remote control.

items.title: 'Lorem Ipsum' Malware Pivots to ClickFix Delivery
items.description: New analysis shows the campaign, which uses compromised WordPress sites, may be linked to the ransomware and data extortion group Vice Society.

items.title: iRhythm Confirms Data Stolen in Hack
items.description: The digital health company said it learned of the breach on June 8 and the attackers demanded a ransom. The post iRhythm Confirms Data Stolen in Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
items.description: Opening a new social media account in the UK will soon mean proving you're over 16 with an ID upload or a facial age scan, under a government ban on under-16s taking effect in spring 2027. Security experts warn the age checks are easy to circumvent and create new data-breach risks. [...]

items.title: Hacker Conversations: Isira Adithya, the Evolution of an Ethical Hacker
items.description: From building LED bulbs to graduating college and buying a house with money earned from bug bounties. The post Hacker Conversations: Isira Adithya, the Evolution of an Ethical Hacker appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware
items.description: GhostTree uses recursive NTFS junctions to generate vast numbers of valid Windows file paths. Varonis explains how the technique could cause Microsoft Defender folder scans to never complete, leaving malware undetected. [...]

items.title: FTC warns of record $3.5 billion losses to imposter scams in 2025
items.description: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC) warned that Americans lost $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, with reported losses nearly tripling since 2020. [...]

items.title: Magnitude Emerges From Stealth Mode With $10 Million in Funding
items.description: The company is enhancing third-party risk management (TPRM) through autonomous AI agents. The post Magnitude Emerges From Stealth Mode With $10 Million in Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI and Cybersecurity – Everything You Wanted to Know, But Were Afraid to Ask
items.description: From defending networks to enabling attacks, artificial intelligence is changing every aspect of cybersecurity. Here's what dozens of experts say security leaders need to understand now. The post AI and Cybersecurity – Everything You Wanted to Know, But Were Afraid to Ask appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
items.description: Security researchers at Zimperium's zLabs have documented a new Android banking trojan, Rokarolla, that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and packs 137 remote commands. Together, they give an operator near-total control of an infected phone: it lifts lock-screen PINs, reads and sends SMS, rewrites the clipboard to redirect crypto payments, and switches off Google Play

items.title: Endpoint Security Startup Ent Emerges From Stealth With $100 Million Seed Round
items.description: Ent has developed an intent-aware platform designed to interpret user and agent behavior before risky actions are carried out. The post Endpoint Security Startup Ent Emerges From Stealth With $100 Million Seed Round appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybercrime Group Claims Novo Nordisk Hack
items.description: The hack-and-leak group FulcrumSec claims to have stolen 1.3TB of data from the pharmaceutical giant. The post Cybercrime Group Claims Novo Nordisk Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Can CISOs Trust Their Applications? TrustCloud Wants to Replace the Questionnaire
items.description: By continuously analyzing security, infrastructure, and governance data, TrustCloud aims to give CISOs a real-time view of application risk and board-ready assurance. The post Can CISOs Trust Their Applications? TrustCloud Wants to Replace the Questionnaire appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims
items.description: California Water Service says there is no indication of operational disruptions to its water and wastewater systems. The post Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: White House Issues Memo to Bolster NSS Cybersecurity
items.description: NSPM-12 establishes a clear structure for NSS cybersecurity governance and accountability and reestablishes CNSS. The post White House Issues Memo to Bolster NSS Cybersecurity appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Survey: 94% of Incidents Involve Anonymized Infrastructure. Teams Are Still Reactive
items.description: Security teams have never had more IP data at their disposal. Every day, analysts ingest enrichment feeds, geolocation data, reputation scores, telemetry, and threat intelligence from a growing ecosystem of vendors and platforms. Yet despite this abundance of information, many organizations continue to face a fundamental challenge: sifting through the noise to understand who is behind an IP and

items.title: Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages
items.description: Arch Linux suspended account registrations in response to the wave of malicious packages being uploaded to AUR. The post Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has given U.S. government agencies three days to secure their servers against an actively exploited vulnerability (CVE-2026-54420) in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin. [...]

items.title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
items.description: Bad actors are exploiting multiple security vulnerabilities in Fortinet FortiSandbox, according to threat intelligence firm Defused Cyber. In a post shared on X, the company said it has observed exploitation of CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 over the past 24 hours. CVE-2026-39813 (CVSS score: 9.1) refers to a path traversal vulnerability in FortiSandbox JRPC API that could

items.title: Ransomware gang abuses Microsoft Teams relays to hide malicious traffic
items.description: DragonForce ransomware used a custom malware named 'Backdoor.Turn' to hide command-and-control traffic inside Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure. [...]

items.title: China-Linked SprySOCKS Backdoor Expands to Windows with Driver-Based Stealth
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged two previously undocumented Windows variants of what was believed to be a Linux-only backdoor called SprySOCKS. "The Windows variants discovered are internally marked as WIN_DRV and WIN_PLUS," ESET said in a report shared with The Hacker News. "Both come with a hard-coded C&C [command-and-control] configuration and support communication over TCP, UDP,

items.title: Critical Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws now exploited in attacks
items.description: Attackers are now exploiting several critical vulnerabilities in Fortinet's FortiSandbox cyber threat detection platform, according to threat intelligence company Defused. [...]

items.title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
items.description: Windows variants for the SprySOCKS Linux malware have been used in attacks targeting government organizations in at least four countries. [...]

items.title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
items.description: The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group known as ScarCruft (aka APT37) has been observed using spear-phishing messages impersonating Microsoft Account security notifications to deliver malware called NarwhalRAT. "The attack email contained a message impersonating an MS account security alert," the Genians Security Center (GSC) said. "It was designed to create concern over possible

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
items.description: Cisco has released security updates for a medium-severity security flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that has come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20262, carries a CVSS score of 6.5 out of 10.0. "A vulnerability in the web UI of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, formerly SD-WAN vManage, could allow an authenticated, remote attacker to create a file or

items.title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a security flaw impacting LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, requiring Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 18, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-54420 (CVSS score: 8.5), which has been described as a case of privilege

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 16th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9974, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
items.description: A China-linked espionage group hid inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks for more than a year, quietly stealing sensitive research and defense email. The way in was a backdoor on their REDCap research servers that stole login credentials. The exfiltration was the unusual part: the attackers rewired the victims' own Google Workspace rules to copy any message

items.title: HTTP/2 Bomb Attacks Put Telcos, Healthcare Orgs at Risk
items.description: The denial-of-service (DoS) exploit takes advantage of two features in HTTP/2 that were designed to save Internet bandwith, not power massive amplification attacks.

items.title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
items.description: The critical, three-stage attack is now patched, but it's part of a new group of AI prompt-injection issues that use hidden URLs and other variables.

items.title: China-Nexus Actor Spies on US Researchers Undetected for a Year
items.description: Google discovered and disrupted the sprawling campaign, which stole RedCAP credentials to breach numerous institutions and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: Most CISOs Report Pressure to Bury Bad Security News
items.description: Executive leaders may not be saying it aloud, but business objectives and priorities don't always promote timely disclosures.

items.title: The Beginning of the End of Social Engineering
items.description: AI-native operating systems are shifting the responsibility to stay vigilant against social engineering cyberattacks from the user onto the system itself.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9968, (Thu, Jun 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
